跳转到导航栏跳转到搜索对角线（Ꝃ，ꝃ）的K是拉丁字母的字母，源自K，并在腿部增加了对角线。
   该字母在中世纪文本中用作kalendas，calends以及karta和kartam（文档或文书）的缩写。 [1] [2]也可以通过“带笔划的K”（Ꝁ，ꝁ）或“带笔划和对角线的K”（Ꝅ，ꝅ）来执行相同的功能。 [1]
 在布列塔尼语中，主要在15世纪至20世纪使用此字母来缩写Ker [3]。
   从5.1版开始，带有对角线笔迹的大写字母K和小K用Unicode编码，编码点为U + A742和U + A743。 [4] [5]

^ Andries，Patrick（2003-03-25）。 “与Unicode联盟技术总监Ken Whistler的访谈”。数字文档（法语）。 6（3-4）：329-351。 doi：10.3166 / dn.6.3-4.329-351。 ISSN 1279-5127。
note_en:
Jump to navigation  Jump to search  K with diagonal stroke (Ꝃ, ꝃ) is a letter of the  Latin alphabet, derived from  K with the addition of a diagonal  bar through the leg.
   This letter is used in medieval texts as an abbreviation for   kalendas, calends, as well as for  karta and  kartam, a document or writ.  [1]  [2] The same function could also be performed by " K with stroke" (Ꝁ, ꝁ), or " K with stroke and diagonal stroke" (Ꝅ, ꝅ).  [1]
 In the  Breton language, this letter is used, mainly from the fifteenth to the twentieth century, to abbreviate  Ker  [3].
   Capital and small K with diagonal stroke is encoded in  Unicode as of version 5.1, at codepoints U+A742 and U+A743.  [4]  [5]

note:
通过在加压室内进行氧气治疗，衰老过程首次在生物学上得以逆转。
 以色列的科学家表明，他们可以逆转身体中两个关键区域的时光，这些区域被认为是造成老年人衰老和身体虚弱的原因。
 随着年龄的增长，染色体末端的保护帽（称为端粒）会缩短，导致DNA受损且细胞停止复制。同时，“僵尸”衰老细胞在体内积累，阻止了再生。
 增加长绒毛长度和摆脱衰老细胞是许多抗衰老研究的重点，目前正在针对这些领域开发药物。
 现在，特拉维夫大学的科学家表明，在高压舱中向老年人提供纯氧会使他们的端粒长度增加20％，这是前所未有的壮举。
科学家们说，这种增长可能意味着试验参与者的端粒现在与25年前一样长。
   该疗法还使衰老细胞减少了多达37％，为新的健康细胞再生提供了空间。动物研究表明，去除衰老细胞可将剩余寿命延长三分之一以上。
 “端粒缩短被认为是衰老生物学的“圣杯”，因此正在广泛探索许多药理和环境干预措施，以期实现端粒延长。”特拉维夫大学。
 在这些独特的实验方案中和之后，端粒长度的显着改善为科学界提供了新的认识基础，即衰老确实可以在基本的细胞生物学水平上靶向和逆转。”
 现在，许多科学家认为衰老本身是造成诸如阿尔茨海默氏症，帕金森氏症，关节炎，癌症，心脏病和糖尿病等主要疾病的原因。
还众所周知，肥胖，吸烟，缺乏体育锻炼，维生素缺乏和发炎会加快端粒的缩短，这表明端粒对寿命有重大影响。
  该试验包括35位64岁及以上的健康独立成年人，他们没有进行任何生活方式，饮食或药物调整。每位患者在三个月中每周放置五天，放置在高压舱中90分钟，同时通过面罩呼吸100％的氧气。
 加压室允许更多的氧气溶解到组织中，并模仿“缺氧”或缺氧的状态，这种状态已知具有再生作用。
 先前的试验表明，饮食健康可以保留端粒的长度，而高强度训练六个月已被证明可使端粒延长多达5％。
 以色列小组以前也证明了加压氧气疗法可以改善认知能力下降。
Sagol高压医学与研究中心首席医学研究官Amir Hadanny博士说：“到目前为止，已经证明，改变生活方式和剧烈运动等干预措施对预期的端粒长度缩短具有一定的抑制作用。”
 “但是，在我们的研究中值得注意的是，在短短的三个月的治疗中，我们能够实现如此显着的端粒延长-远远超过了目前任何可用的干预措施或生活方式的改变。
 “通过这项开创性研究，我们为长期治疗细胞以逆转衰老过程的影响打开了进一步的大门。在致力于研究其对脑功能和与年龄相关的认知下降领域的影响之后，我们将现在已经首次在人类中发现健康衰老成年人在细胞水平上的生物学效应。”
note_en:
The ageing process has been biologically reversed for the first time by giving humans oxygen therapy in a pressurised chamber.
 Scientists in Israel showed they could turn back the clock in two key areas of the body believed to be responsible for the frailty and ill-health that comes with growing older.
 As people age, the protective caps at the ends of chromosomes – called telomeres – shorten, causing DNA to become damaged and cells to stop replicating. At the same time, "zombie" senescent cells build up in the body, preventing regeneration.
 Increasing telemere length and getting rid of senescent cells is the focus of many anti-ageing studies, and  drugs are being developed to target those areas.
 Now scientists at Tel Aviv University have shown that giving pure oxygen to older people while in a hyperbaric chamber increased the length of their telomeres by 20 per cent, a feat that has never been achieved before.
 Scientists said the growth may mean that the telomeres of trial participants were now as long as they had been 25 years earlier.
   The therapy also reduced senescent cells by up to 37 per cent, making way for new healthy cells to regrow. Animal studies have shown that removing senescent cells extends remaining life by more than one third.
 "Since telomere shortening is considered the 'Holy Grail' of the biology of ageing, many pharmacological and environmental interventions are being extensively explored in the hopes of enabling telomere elongation," said Professor Shai Efrati of the Faculty of Medicine and Sagol School of Neuroscience at Tel Aviv University.
 "The significant improvement of telomere length shown during and after these unique protocols provides the scientific community with a new foundation of understanding that ageing can indeed be targeted and reversed at the basic cellular-biological level."
 Many scientists now believe ageing itself is responsible for major conditions such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, arthritis, cancer, heart disease, and diabetes.
  It is also known that obesity, smoking, lack of physical activity, vitamin deficiency and inflammation can speed up the shortening of telomeres, demonstrating that they have a major impact on longevity.
  The trial included 35 healthy independent adults aged 64 and older who did not undergo any lifestyle, diet or medication adjustments. Each patient was placed in a hyperbaric chamber for 90 minutes for five days a week over three months while breathing 100 per cent oxygen through a mask.
 The pressurised chamber allows more oxygen to be dissolved into the tissues and mimics a state of "hypoxia", or oxygen shortage, which is known to have regenerating effects.
 Previous trials have shown that eating a  healthy diet can preserve telomere length, while high-intensity training for six months has been proven to lengthen telomeres by up to five per cent.
 The Israeli team has also previously demonstrated that the pressurised oxygen therapy can improve cognitive decline.
 "Until now, interventions such as lifestyle modifications and  intense exercise were shown to have some inhibition effect on the expected telomere length shortening," said Dr Amir Hadanny, chief medical research officer of the Sagol Center for Hyperbaric Medicine and Research.
 "However, what is remarkable to note in our study is that, in just three months of therapy, we were able to achieve such significant telomere elongation – at rates far beyond any of the current available interventions or lifestyle modifications.
 "With this pioneering study, we have opened a door for further research on the prolonged cellular impact of the therapy to reverse the ageing process. After dedicating our research to exploring its impact on the areas of brain functionality and age-related cognitive decline, we have now uncovered, for the first time in humans, biological effects at the cellular level in healthy ageing adults."
title: 人类衰老过程在生物学上首创
title_en: Human ageing process biologically reversed in world first

Their conclusion: Borrowers will pay back $935 billion in principal and interest. That would leave taxpayers on the hook for $435 billion, according to documents...

note:
俄罗斯黑客组织REvil声称对电信传播家肯尼斯·科普兰（Kenneth Copeland）进行了大规模攻击-后者以怪异的假笑回应了拜登的胜利-并威胁说，如果他拒绝支付赎金，将释放1.2 TB的“敏感”数据
         被称为REvil的俄罗斯黑客组织已将电视传播家肯尼斯·科普兰（Kenneth Copeland）指定为其最新受害者，威胁说如果他拒绝支付未指定的赎金要求，将释放1.2兆字节的敏感数据。
 雷维尔周三发表声明说，它已经接管了肯尼思·科普兰各部的服务器，肯尼斯·科普兰各部是总部位于德克萨斯州的著名83岁牧师的国际教堂。
 该组织在《 Dark Web》上的声明中说，该公司的所有服务器和正常工作的计算机绝对都是被黑客攻击和加密的。
 REvil发布了一个文件库的图像，目的是显示被盗的数据，并声称该信息包括“财务文件，合同，银行文件，销售历史和[和]电子邮件”。
Copeland于1967年成立了该部，并曾担任唐纳德·特朗普总统的信仰顾问委员会成员，据报道其净资产在3亿至7.6亿美元之间。
  REvil在公开声明中没有具体说明其财务需求，该声明已于周四删除，这可能表明已与受害者进行了谈判。
  REvil的签名策略-加密公司的服务器，然后威胁要发布或拍卖其数据-是勒索软件组织中越来越普遍的骗局。
 勒索软件组织在选择受害者方面是不分青红皂白的。网络安全公司Emsisoft的威胁分析师布雷特·卡洛（Brett Callow）告诉DailyMail.com。
 卡洛补充说：“勒索软件正变得越来越成为一个问题。”需求在增加，攻击的复杂性也在增加，这些组织部署的策略也越来越极端。
谷轮上周登上头条新闻，当时他出现在一部离奇的录像带中，在那里他假笑了近一分钟，嘲笑媒体呼吁2020年总统大选胜过拜登。
     谷轮还因其关于冠状病毒大流行的言论而受到批评，他一再声称已经过或即将结束。
 3月，牧师对该病毒“执行了判断”，并宣布其“完成”，同时要求“立即进行疫苗接种”。
 黑客组织REvil以前针对的是名人律师事务所Grubman，Shire，Meiselas＆Sacks，后者拒绝支付REvil要求的数百万赎金。
 REvil从律师事务所的黑客中向特朗普承诺的“破坏性”细节从未实现。
在最近一次对俄语语言技术博客的采访中，据称是REvil的代表，该组织最成功的攻击方法是通过远程桌面协议，该协议允许通过Internet远程管理桌面。
 安全专家建议在公司的计算机上禁用RDP，并说，针对黑客的最佳保护是快速，频繁地使用最新更新来更新软件。
 网络安全专家卡洛（Callow）估计，每年大约有17亿美元是以黑客赎金形式支付的，他说，包括停机时间在内的攻击总成本接近100亿美元。
 他说，解决这个问题的唯一方法是禁止支付赎金。 '如果现金流停止，攻击也将停止。就这么简单。
note_en:
Russian hacker group REvil claims massive attack on televangelist Kenneth Copeland - who responded to Biden victory with bizarre fake laugh - and threatens to release 1.2 terrabytes of 'sensitive' data if he refuses to pay their ransom
         The Russian hacker group known as REvil has named televangelist Kenneth Copeland as its latest victim, threatening to release 1.2 terrabytes of sensitive data if he declines to pay their unspecified ransom demands.
 REvil on Wednesday issued a statement saying it had taken over the servers of Kenneth Copeland Ministries, the Texas-based international church of the prominent 83-year-old pastor.
 'Absolutely all servers and working computers of the company are hacked and encrypted,' the group said in the statement on the Dark Web, which was reviewed by DailyMail.com.
 REvil released images of a file library purporting to show the stolen data, and claiming the information included 'financial documents, contracts, bank documents, sales history, [and] emails.'
     Copeland, who founded the ministry in 1967 and sat on President Donald Trump's faith advisory board, has a reported net worth of between $300 million and $760 million.
  REvil did not specify their financial demand in the public statement, which was removed on Thursday, possibly indicating negotiations had been entered with the victim.
  REvil's signature tactic -- encrypting a company's servers and then threatening to release or auction off their data -- is an increasingly common scam among ransomware groups.
 'Ransomware groups are indiscriminate in their choice of victims. They’ll attack any body they believe can afford to pay, whether it’s a private company, government, hospital, charity or religious organization,' Brett Callow, a threat analyst at cybersecurity firm Emsisoft, told DailyMail.com.
 'Ransomware is becoming ever more of a problem,' Callow added. 'The demands are increasing, the sophistication of attacks is increasing and tactics the groups deploy are becoming more extreme.'
 Copeland made headlines last week, when he appeared in a bizarre video where he forced a fake laugh for almost a minute as he mocked the media for calling the 2020 presidential election in favor of Joe Biden.
     Copeland has also drawn criticism for his statements about the coronavirus pandemic, which he repeatedly claimed had passed or would soon end.
 In March, the pastor 'executed judgment' on the virus and declared it to be 'finished' while demanding 'a vaccination to come immediately.'
 The hacker group REvil previously targeted celebrity law firm Grubman, Shire, Meiselas & Sacks, which  refused to pay the millions in ransom that REvil demanded.
 The 'damaging' details against Trump that REvil had promised from the law firm hack never materialized.
  In a recent interview with a Russian-language tech blog, a purported representative of REvil said that the group's most successful method of attack was through Remote Desktop Protocol, which allows for remote administration of a desktop over the internet.
 Security experts recommend disabling RDP on company computers, and say that the best protection against hackers is quickly and frequently updating software with the latest updates.
 Callow, the cybersecurity expert, estimated that almost $1.7 billion is paid annually in hacker ransoms, saying the total cost of attacks including downtime approaches $10 billion.
 'The only way to deal with the problem is to prohibit the payment of ransoms,' he said. 'If the flow of cash stops, the attacks will stop too. It’s that simple.'
title: 俄罗斯黑客组织REvil宣称对电信传播家肯尼斯·科普兰（Kenneth Copeland）的攻击
title_en: Russian hacker group REvil claims attack on televangelist Kenneth Copeland

note:
等待时间很长，但本周时间是对的：Airbnb终于提交了S-1，Affirm，C3.ai，Roblox和Wish也提交了。在技​​术IPO已经是最高级的一年结束之前，我们很可能会在公开市场上看到这五个价格。显然，持续的大流行和政治动荡还不够可怕。
 在接下来的十年中，您必须考虑到，我们会看到越来越多的科技公司上市。多年来，许多上述公司都落后于私人投资增长战略。但是，今天，该行业对SPAC和直接上市以及各种融资途径有了更好的了解。公司从成立之日起就拥有更多选择，帮助他们如何成长和退出一天。公众投资者对于2020年科技公司的当前收入数字和未来增长机会似乎也有了更深的了解。为什么，我仍然记得所有不久前吹嘘做空Facebook IPO的天才。
  我们会看到IPO的来源更加均匀吗？尽管本周所有文件提交人的总部都设在旧金山或周边地区，但现在看来，这些公司成立的年代简直是巧合。越来越多的州一直在铸造自己的独角兽，总部位于俄亥俄州的Root Insurance最近公开上市，而位于犹他州的Qualtrics则以这种方式发展。与此同时，科技初创企业现在已遍布全球，许多国家都在努力使独角兽比纽约更靠近本国。
               乔·拜登（Joe Biden）担任技术政策总裁的打算是什么？一方面，科技公司可能不会太快地回到白宫。泰勒·哈特梅克（Taylor Hatmaker）解释说：“总的来说，我们看到了一些熟悉的名字，但是2020年不是2008年，” “在过去的十年中成为金童的科技公司现在具有放射性。监管在各个方向上隐约可见。无论拜登政府提出什么政策优先事项，奥巴马的技术官僚镀金时代已经过去，我们正在寻求新的东西。”
 但是，专注于共同目标的高科技行业和公司可能会获得支持。在对拜登的气候变化政策进行回顾时，乔恩·希伯（Jon Shieber）研究了可能正在实施的主要绿色基础设施计划。
拜登政府制定的任何政策都必须广泛地关注经济机会，而竞选中提出的许多计划都可以满足这一需求。它的主要主张之一是“在落后的社区创造良好的，工会的，中产阶级的工作，纠正污染首当其冲的社区的错误，并在整个伟大国家（农村，城市和部落”，根据过渡网站。尽早强调电网和公用事业基础设施可能会为全美创造就业机会创造重大机遇，并为科技公司带来动力。以能源和气候为重点的风险投资公司Congruent Ventures的联合创始人安倍晋三（Abe Yokell）说：“我们的电力基础设施陈旧，老化且不安全。” “从基础设施的角度来看，输配电的确需要升级，并且多年来投资不足。而且，这与在全美提供可再生能源的部署以及一切电气化都息息相关。”
   今年技术工人短缺是建筑业传统挑战的重中之重。贝恩资本风险投资公司的艾莉森·许（Allison Xu）在本周的Extra Crunch客座专栏中写道，其结果是，技术的采用正在推动现实世界的发展。她列出了技术初创公司正在兴起的六个主要建筑类别，包括项目构想，设计和工程，施工前，施工执行，施工后和施工管理。这是有关最后一项的文章摘录：
 今天的工作方式：建筑管理和运营团队管理端到端项目，其功能包括文档管理，数据和见解，会计，财务，人力资源/薪资等。
 关键挑战：工作现场的复杂性转化为与每个项目相关的高度复杂且繁琐的文书工作。管理流程需要许多利益相关者之间的沟通和协调。
 技术如何应对挑战：多方利益相关方建设过程的细微差别在纵向管理项目中值得价值。 Procore，Hyphen Solutions和IngeniousIO等施工管理工具为承包商提供了无缝衔接和跟踪端到端过程的方法。 Levelset等其他参与者也采用了特定于建筑的方法来处理发票管理和付款等功能。
大流行时代的工作解决方案（例如在线团队会议空间）正朝着不太确定的，基于疫苗的现实发展。我们是否都偏远偏远，以至于他们仍然拥有真正的市场？ Natasha Mascarenhas向一些顶尖公司查询了一下它的外观，还有更多：
 为了使远程工作更加自发，有数十家新创公司正在努力为分布式团队创建虚拟总部。排名前三的是Z世代玩家打造的Branch。 Gather，由工程师打造的游戏化Zoom创建；和杂乱无章的人，仍然在隐身。
 这些平台都在竞相证明世界已经准备好成为虚拟工作区的一部分。通过借鉴多人游戏文化，初创企业正在使用空间技术，动画和生产力工具来创建致力于工作的元节。
 未来最大的挑战？初创公司需要说服风险投资家和用户，他们不只是企业版Sims或始终在线的Zoom电话。潜在的成功可能预示着工作的未来将如何将游戏和社交化融合到分布式团队中。
                     您好，欢迎回到TechCrunch专注于风险资本的播客Equity（现在在Twitter上！），我们在头条新闻后面整理了数字。
这个星期忙得不可开交。还有什么，包括Airbnb和Affirm IPO申请在内的一周，一系列针对新独角兽的大型融资活动，一些引人入胜的小型筹资活动和一些新基金？
 因此我们经历了很多事情，但与克里斯，丹尼，娜塔莎和你谦卑的仆人一起，我们做到了头等舱：
 申明已申请公开！金融科技独角兽规模庞大，随着时间的流逝不断增长且亏损减少。我们的第一眼印象非常深刻。然后，花了更多时间，我们更深入地挖掘并发现了一两个弱点。尽管如此，Affirm仍在走向公众，并且状况不佳。
 Airbnb提起了诉讼，我们在周二尽可能快地跳入了Equity Shot，以使我们对新闻感兴趣。从那时起，丹尼（Danny）在风险投资赢家圈子里挖了一个令人惊讶的小公司！ -并且我们也对公司的财务状况提出了一些疑问。
 据说Robinhood在账簿上有IPO，所以我们谈论了一些有关其第三季度增长的信息。
然后像往常一样出现了edtech。本周我们讨论了腾讯支持Udemy，Duolingo再次筹集资金以及Transfr挑选了我们认为非常有趣的A轮融资。
 丹尼想谈谈《信任与意志》系列A.我们试图避免这么说笑话。
  金融创业工作室（Financial Venture Studio）成立了一支新基金，以将小额支票切入种子期的金融科技初创公司。我们认为那太好了。尤其是考虑到我们对金融科技风险投资世界的了解。
 娜塔莎（Natasha）带领我们完成了最新的深度学习，深入了解了虚拟总部的世界。这导致了演出中最糟糕的笑话。
 好一个星期！三集，一些新的唱片，并且令我们感到非常疲倦。周一还有更多！
每周一下午7点（太平洋标准时间）和周四下午，资产净值会尽快下降，因此请订阅Apple Podcast，Overcast，Spotify和所有演员表。
note_en:
The wait was long but this week the time was right: Airbnb finally filed its S-1 and so did Affirm, C3.ai, Roblox, and Wish. We are likely to see these five price on public markets before the end of an already superlative year for tech IPOs. The ongoing pandemic and political turmoil were not scary enough, apparently.
 This coming decade, you have to think that we’ll see a more even spread of tech companies going public. Many of the companies above have been bottled up for years behind privately funded growth strategies. Today, however, the industry has a better grasp of SPACs and direct listings, and various funding routes. Companies have more options from their founding for how they might grow and exit one day. Public investors in 2020 also seem to have a deeper appreciation for the current revenue numbers and future growth opportunities for tech companies. Why, I can still remember all the geniuses who bragged about shorting the Facebook IPO not so long ago.
  Will we see a more even spread of where IPOs come from? While all of this week’s filers are headquartered in San Francisco or environs, that now feels almost like a coincidental reference to the years when these companies were founded. More states have been minting their own unicorns, with Ohio-based Root Insurance recently going public and Utah-based Qualtrics heading (back) that way. Tech startups are now global, meanwhile, and plenty of countries are working to keep their unicorns closer to home than New York.
               What does Joe Biden intend as president around technology policy? On the one hand, tech companies might not be returning to the White House too fast. “All told, we’re seeing some familiar names in the mix, but 2020 isn’t 2008,”  Taylor Hatmaker explains about potential presidential appointments from the industry. “Tech companies that emerged as golden children over the last 10 years are radioactive now. Regulation looms on the horizon in every direction. Whatever policy priorities emerge out of the Biden administration, Obama’s technocratic gilded age is over and we’re in for something new.”
 However, tech industries and companies focused on shared goals might find support. In a  review of Biden’s climate-change policies, Jon Shieber looks at major green infrastructure plans that could be on the way.
 Any policies that a Biden administration enacts would have to focus on economic opportunity broadly, and much of the proposed plan from the campaign fulfills that need. One of its key propositions was that it would be “creating good, union, middle-class jobs in communities left behind, righting wrongs in communities that bear the brunt of pollution, and lifting up the best ideas from across our great nation — rural, urban and tribal,” according to the transition website. An early emphasis on grid and utility infrastructure could create significant opportunities for job creation across America — and be a boost for technology companies. “Our electric power infrastructure is old, aging and not secure,” said Abe Yokell, co-founder of the energy and climate-focused venture capital firm Congruent Ventures. “From an infrastructure standpoint, transmission distribution really should be upgraded and has been underinvested over the years. And it is in direct alignment with providing renewable energy deployment across the U.S. and the electrification of everything.”
   A skilled labor shortage is piling on top of the construction industry’s traditional challenges this year. The result is that tech adoption is getting a big push into the real world, Allison Xu of Bain Capital Ventures writes in a guest column for Extra Crunch this week. She maps out six main  construction categories where tech startups are emerging, including project conception, design and engineering, pre-construction, construction execution, post construction and construction management. Here’s an excerpt from the article about that last item:
 How it works today: Construction management and operations teams manage the end-to-end project, with functions such as document management, data and insights, accounting, financing, HR/payroll, etc.
 Key challenges: The complexity of the job site translates to highly complex and burdensome paperwork associated with each project. Managing the process requires communication and alignment across many stakeholders.
 How technology can address challenges: The nuances of the multistakeholder construction process merit value in a verticalized approach to managing the project. Construction management tools like  Procore,  Hyphen Solutions and  IngeniousIO have created ways for contractors to coordinate and track the end-to-end process more seamlessly. Other players like  Levelset have taken a construction-specific approach to functions like invoice management and payments.
   Pandemic-era work solutions like online team meeting spaces are heading towards a less certain, vaccine-based reality. Have we all gone remote-first enough that they will have a real market, still?  Natasha Mascarenhas checks in with some of the top companies to see how it’s looking, here’s more:
 With the goal of making remote work more spontaneous, there are dozens of new startups working to create virtual HQs for distributed teams. The three that have risen to the top include  Branch, built by Gen Z gamers;  Gather, created by engineers building a gamified Zoom; and  Huddle, which is still in stealth.
 The platforms are all racing to prove that the world is ready to be a part of virtual workspaces. By drawing on multiplayer gaming culture, the startups are using spatial technology, animations and productivity tools to create a metaverse dedicated to work.
 The biggest challenge ahead? The startups need to convince venture capitalists and users alike that they’re more than Sims for Enterprise or an always-on Zoom call. The potential success could signal how the future of work will blend gaming and socialization for distributed teams.
                     Hello and welcome back to  Equity, TechCrunch’s venture capital-focused podcast ( now on Twitter!), where we unpack the numbers behind the headlines.
 This week wound up being incredibly busy. What else, with a week that included both the Airbnb and Affirm IPO filings, a host of mega-rounds for new unicorns, some fascinating smaller funding events and some new funds?
 So we had a lot to get through, but with  Chris and  Danny and  Natasha and  your humble servant, we dove in headfirst:
 Affirm has filed to go public! The fintech unicorn is big, growing and losing less money over time. We were pretty impressed  in our first look. Then, with a bit more time, we  dug deeper and found a weakness or two. Still, Affirm is heading public and not in poor shape.
 Airbnb filed, and we jumped into  an Equity Shot as fast as we could on Tuesday to get our minds around the news. Since then, Danny dug  through the venture capital winners circle — a surprisingly small subset of firms! — and we also  got into some questions that I had about the company’s finances.
 Robinhood is said to have an IPO in the books, so we talked a bit about  what we know concerning its Q3 growth.
 And then there was edtech, as always. This week we talked about  Tencent backing Udemy,  Duolingo raising again and  Transfr picking up a Series A that we thought was super interesting.
 Danny wanted to talk about  the Trust & Will Series A. We tried to not make that many jokes.
  Financial Venture Studio  put together a new fund to cut small checks into seed-stage fintech startups. We think that’s great. Especially given what we know about what is going on in the fintech venture world.
 And Natasha  walked us through her latest deep-dive, a look into the world of virtual headquarters. This led to the worst joke of the show.
 What a week! Three episodes, some new records, and a very tired us after all the action. More on Monday!
 Equity drops every Monday at 7:00 a.m. PDT and Thursday afternoon as fast as we can get it out, so subscribe to us on  Apple Podcasts,  Overcast,  Spotify and all the casts.
title: Affirm，Airbnb，C3.ai，Roblox，Wish申请2020年技术IPO结局

YouTube-dl的源代码（可用于从YouTube下载视频的工具）已在代码存储库于10月因美国唱片业协会（RIAA）的DMCA投诉而被注销后在GitHub上备份。 GitHub引用了电子前沿基金会（EFF）的一封信，称其最终发现RIAA的投诉没有任何根据。
 RIAA辩称，该工具违反了美国版权法的第1201条，因为该工具为人们提供了规避YouTube DRM的手段。它还声称该软件的文档邀请人们盗版几首流行歌曲。 EFF在信中剖析了RIAA的主张，强调了该组织在哪里误解了法律或YouTube-dl的代码是如何工作的。该组织指出，“重要的是，YouTube-dl不会解密使用诸如Netflix之类的订阅视频网站使用的商业DRM技术（例如Widevine）加密的视频流，”该组织指出了RIAA的主要主张。
  这是RIAA攻击youtube-dl的最佳结果。在@GitHub上不错，可以支持开发人员抵御DMCA§1201的滥用。 @EFF代表该项目做了出色的工作，您应该阅读他们的来信：https://t.co/Whh0cKTgIF https://t.co/BT1aovWZx7
-Filippo Valsordada❤️✊（@FiloSottile）2020年11月16日
  如果这一集有一线希望，那就是GitHub正在实施新政策，以避免重复出现的情况继续发展。首先，它说，由技术和法律专家组成的团队将手动评估1201部分的每个索赔。该公司表示，如果对索赔有任何歧义，它将错在开发人员的一边，并将其存储库保留在线状态。如果该公司的技术和法律团队最终发现某个项目有任何问题，那么GitHub将为它的所有者提供在解决其问题之前解决这些问题的机会。删除之后，只要不包含任何令人反感的代码，它将继续为人们提供恢复数据的机会。
GitHub还建立了一个100万美元的国防基金，以针对可疑的1201部分索赔向开发人员提供法律援助，并加倍其游说工作以修改DMCA和全球其他类似的版权法。
note_en:
The source code for YouTube-dl, a tool you can use to download videos from YouTube, is  back up on GitHub after the code repository  took it down in October following a DMCA complaint from the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA). Citing a  letter from the Electronic Frontier Foundation (the EFF), GitHub says it ultimately found that the RIAA’s complaint didn’t have any merit.
 The RIAA argued the tool ran afoul of section 1201 of the US copyright law by giving people the means to circumvent YouTube’s DRM. It also claimed the software’s documentation invited people to pirate several popular songs. In its letter, the EFF dissects the RIAA’s claims, highlighting where the organization had either misinterpreted the law or how the code of YouTube-dl works. “Importantly, YouTube-dl does not decrypt video streams that are encrypted with commercial DRM technologies, such as Widevine, that are used by subscription videos sites, such as Netflix,” the organization points out when it comes to the RIAA’s primary claim.
  This is the best possible outcome of the RIAA's attack on youtube-dl. Good on  @GitHub for standing up for developers against DMCA § 1201 abuses.  The  @EFF did amazing work representing the project, and you should read their letter:  https://t.co/Whh0cKTgIF https://t.co/BT1aovWZx7
— Filippo Valsorda 💚🤍❤️ ✊ (@FiloSottile)  November 16, 2020
  If there’s a silver lining to the episode, it’s that GitHub is implementing new policies to avoid a repeat of a repeat situation moving forward. First, it says a team of both technical and legal experts will manually evaluate every single section 1201 claim. In instances where there’s any ambiguity to a claim, the company says it will err on the side of developers and leave their repository online. If the company’s technical and legal teams ultimately find any issues with a project, GitHub will give its owners the chance to address those problems before it takes down their work. Following a takedown, it will continue to give people the chance to recover their data — provided it doesn’t include any offending code.
 GitHub is also establishing a $1 million defense fund to provide legal aid to developers against suspect section 1201 claims, as well as doubling down on its lobbying work to amend the DMCA and other similar copyright laws across the world.
title: 恢复YouTube-dl后，GitHub修订了其版权删除政策
title_en: After Restoring YouTube-dl, GitHub Revamps Its Copyright Takedown Policy

四年级数学上的一天，学生抱怨前一天晚上的作业。
   一位学生说：“妈妈不得不和我一起坐一会儿，但最终我得到了。”另一个学生悄悄地说：“我妈妈没有那样做。”
   第一个学生来自较高的社会经济背景，而第二个来自较低的社会经济背景。这一轶事只是教育学者在一份新的工作论文中提供的数据的一个方面，该论文发现，数学家庭作业加剧了来自不同社会经济背景的学生的不平等待遇。
    加强地位的过程，或弥补先前存在的分歧的过程，在教育中占一角。标准化测试，创建荣誉和AP跟踪以及基于感知能力对学生进行分组都可以帮助缺乏支持结构和与富裕相关的父母参与的处境不利的学生。
   专门研究数学家庭作业，新工作论文的作者想看看家庭作业是否又是强化状态的过程。事实证明，事实证明，研究人员说，为解决家庭作业差距而提供的传统解决方案是行不通的。
该论文的第一作者，印第安纳大学心理学副教授杰西卡·卡拉尔科（Jessica Calarco）说：“在这里，老师们知道学生在功课上得到了不平等的支持。” “但是，由于这些将家庭作业视为学生个人责任的，标准的，被视为准予的政策，它消除了那些不平等的支持背景，并导致教师以这种强化身份的方式来解释和回应家庭作业。”
   在52名白人学生中，来自较高社会经济背景的学生在三年级，四年级和五年级的数学作业中平均表现更好。与来自较低社会经济背景的同龄人相比，他们的GPA和考试成绩也更高。
   在2008年至2012年之间，Calarco参加了课程并在一个郊区学区对学生，护理人员和老师进行了采访。她从该项目中积累了数千页的实地记录和数百小时的采访，这成为她的博士学位论文。当她浏览这些数据时，她说她很惊讶地发现重复的数学作业实例使不平等长期存在，甚至加剧了不平等。
    除了观察教室并随着时间的推移跟踪学生的表现，Calarco还对学生家庭进行了定性访谈，以探讨影响家庭作业完成的因素，并向数学老师进行了关于家庭作业完成如何影响他们对学生看法的数学老师的访谈。
   Calarco和她的合著者发现学生的社会经济状况有多种情况影响他们完成家庭作业的能力：一位较富裕的父母说她聘请了一位辅导老师来帮助四年级学生完成家庭作业，而另一位来自社会经济背景较低的父母解释说她在上社区大学时勉强通过数学，感到“愚蠢”，因为她无法帮助她的五年级学生。
Calarco说，接受论文采访的老师们承认不平等的情境会影响学生能否完整，正确地完成数学作业。但是，这并不能阻止同一位教师将家庭作业作为衡量学生能力的一种方式。
   Calarco说：“对我来说，最令人震惊和困扰的是听到老师因为学生们没有完成作业而注销学生。”
   在该论文中，一位七年级的老师说，一个来自社会经济背景较低的学生，如果他不做家庭作业，那是“没有给她任何可与之合作的东西”。另一方面，具有较高社会经济背景的学生的父母经常通过电子邮件向老师询问有关家庭作业的澄清问题，因而被称赞为“勤奋认真的工作”。
    COVID-19大流行只会加剧学生完成作业的不平等条件。该论文描述了一位要求在其当地图书馆进行在线作业的学生，​​并解释说他和他的家人“不再有互联网”。 Calarco说，在长时间的远程学习期间，她最关心像这样的学生。
      Calarco说，家庭作业之所以能够起到提高地位的作用，部分原因是，当许多其他因素影响结业时，正式的学校政策和分级计划会将其视为衡量学生个人努力和责任感的标准。
根据该论文，专家们传统上认为，鼓励父母更多地参与子女的教育可以提高家庭作业的完成率。该论文称，这样的干预还不够，而且在很多情况下，对于“受过正规教育，财务资源或英语能力有限的父母”来说，这是不可能的。
   分配作业有多种选择，尤其是在小学阶段。 Calarco说，她听说老师会每周发一些时事通讯，其中包含可选的讨论问题，以使父母有与孩子们完成家庭作业相同的课程窗口。
   她说，至少，教育工作者应该认真思考作业的目的和价值。
   “我不确定我是否想完全出来说我们需要完全禁止家庭作业，但是我认为我们需要认真考虑一下我们何时以及如何分配它。”
note_en:
One day in a fourth grade math class, the students complained about the previous night’s homework.
   “My mom had to sit with me for a while, but I got it eventually,” said one student. Another student piped up, quietly, “My mom doesn’t do that.”
   The first student came from a high socioeconomic background, while the second came from a low socioeconomic background. This anecdote is just one facet of the data presented in  a new working paper from education scholars, which found that math homework reinforces the unequal treatment of students who come from different socioeconomic backgrounds.
    Status-reinforcing processes, or ones that fortify pre-existing divides, are a dime a dozen in education.  Standardized testing, creating honors and AP  tracks, and grouping students based on perceived  ability all serve to disadvantage students who lack the support structures and parental engagement associated with affluence.
   Looking specifically at math homework, the authors of the new working paper wanted to see if homework was yet another status-reinforcing process. As it turns out, it was, and researchers say that the traditional solutions offered up to fix the homework gap won’t work.
   “Here, teachers knew that students were getting unequal support with homework,” said Jessica Calarco, the first author of the paper and an associate professor of psychology at Indiana University. “And yet, because of these standard, taken-for-granted policies that treated homework as students' individual responsibilities, it erased those unequal contexts of support and led teachers to interpret and respond to homework in these status-reinforcing ways.”
   In a cohort of 52 white students, those from a higher socioeconomic background fared better on average on their math homework in third, fourth, and fifth grades. They also maintained higher GPAs and test scores than their peers from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.
   Between 2008 and 2012, Calarco sat in on classes and conducted interviews with students, caregivers, and teachers in a single suburban school district. She amassed thousands of pages of field notes and hundreds of hours of interviews from the project, which became her doctoral dissertation. As she went through that data, she said that she was surprised to find repeated examples of math homework perpetuating and even exacerbating inequality.
    In addition to observing classrooms and tracking students’ performance over time, Calarco conducted qualitative interviews with both the families of students about factors affecting homework completion and the math teachers about how homework completion affected their perceptions of students.
   Calarco and her co-authors found multiple instances of students’ socioeconomic statuses impacting their ability to complete homework: one wealthier parent said she hired a tutor to help her fourth-grader with the homework, while another parent from a lower socioeconomic background explained that she barely passed math while attending community college and felt “stupid” that she wasn’t able to help her fifth-grader.
   The teachers interviewed for the paper acknowledged the unequal contexts affecting whether students could complete their math homework fully and correctly, Calarco said. However, that did not stop the same teachers from using homework as a way to measure students’ abilities.
   “The most shocking and troubling part to me was hearing teachers write off students because they didn't get their homework done,” Calarco said.
   In the paper, a seventh-grade teacher said that a student from a lower socioeconomic background who was not turning in his homework was “not giving [her] anything to work with.” On the other hand, a student from a higher socioeconomic background whose parent often emailed teachers to ask clarifying questions about the homework was praised for being a “hard worker and really diligent.”
    The COVID-19 pandemic has only exacerbated the unequal conditions under which students complete their homework. The paper describes one student who asked to do an online assignment at his local library, explaining that he and his family “don’t have internet anymore.” Calarco said that she is most concerned about students like this one during a time of prolonged remote learning.
      Part of the reason why homework can serve as a status-reinforcing process is that formal school policies and grading schemes treat it as a measure of a student’s individual effort and responsibility, when many other factors affect completion, Calarco said.
   According to the paper, experts have traditionally argued that homework completion can be improved by encouraging parents to be more involved in their children’s education. Such an intervention isn’t enough, and in many cases isn’t possible for parents “with limited formal education, financial resources, or English proficiency,” according to the paper.
   There are alternatives to assigning homework, especially in elementary school. Calarco said that she’d heard of teachers sending out weekly newsletters with optional discussion questions to give parents the same window into the curriculum as they would be getting through their children’s homework.
   At the very least, educators should think critically about the purpose and value of homework, she said.
   “I'm not sure I want to completely come out and say that we need to ban homework entirely, but I think we need to really seriously reconsider when and how we assign it.”
title: 研究人员发现，分配作业会加剧班级分歧
title_en: Assigning Homework Exacerbates Class Divides, Researchers Find

note:
来自苹果APL -1.10％和Google的Covid-19联系人跟踪应用程序正进入更多的州，并且有证据表明，只要有足够的人使用它们，它们就可以帮助缓解感染。
  十四个州和华盛顿特区最近采用了公司在其智能手机操作系统中内置的“曝光通知”技术，这是Big Tech在对抗Covid-19方面最重要的贡献。加利福尼亚和其他州正在对该技术进行试验，并可能很快发布。它使测试过Covid-19呈阳性的人提醒其他人，他们以前曾密切接触过，应该采取预防措施或进行测试。
  流行病学家说，随着病例数激增以及在全国范围内推广疫苗的距离还有数月之遥，流行病学说该技术可以帮助减缓病毒的传播，只要得到广泛使用和快速检测。到目前为止，仍有数以百万计的美国人对此表示怀疑，怀疑或根本不知道。
  那就是弗吉尼亚的斗争，弗吉尼亚在8月初成为第一个推出曝光通知应用程序的州。三个月后，名为Covidwise的应用程序的下载量约占弗吉尼亚州850万人口的十分之一。
    负责Covidwise的弗吉尼亚州卫生专员执行顾问杰夫·斯托弗（Jeff Stover）说，病毒的政治化阻碍了提高应用程序采用率的努力。他说：“这无助于说服弗吉尼亚人下载并运行由政府管理的曝光通知应用程序。”
附近的华盛顿特区为公众参与提供了一些希望。得益于苹果和谷歌的一项调整，该地区包括通勤者在内的该地区三分之一的人口已经激活了智能手机功能，这要归功于苹果和谷歌的一项调整，允许公共卫生部门发送推送提醒该技术可用。发送推送警报后，科罗拉多州和马里兰州的使用率也有所提高。
  斯托弗先生说，弗吉尼亚州计划在几周内进行一次更新，以便它可以发送警报并允许Covidwise处理在其他地方（包括附近的华盛顿特区和马里兰州）运行的该技术的版本。
  今年夏天，许多欧洲国家/地区推出了基于Apple-Google技术的应用。根据9月份发表的一篇论文，在瑞士，有1,695人在SwissCovid应用程序中收到已被暴露的通知后致电特别热线，有65位测试结果为阳性的人认为该应用程序发出了鼓励他们参加测试的通知，以鼓励他们进行测试。
  住在苏黎世郊区的保险业高管Armin Betschart记得9月份的一个星期一下午，当时他的SwissCovid应用程序警告他可能存在这种风险。贝特沙特说，这一通知是在一个家庭成员打电话给他的消息前几个小时就传来了一个阳性测试的消息。
  他的测试结果为阴性，但相信自己是否为阳性，并且该暴露来自一个陌生人，该应用程序将是他唯一的警告，即他可能在症状发作之前具有传染性。
尽管承诺了好处，但美国很少有人下载基于状态的应用程序或以其他方式在手机上启用该技术。斯托弗说，迄今为止，已有约80万人下载了弗吉尼亚州的应用程序。根据研究公司Sensor Tower的数据，纽约的应用程序已被下载约70万次，宾夕法尼亚州则有50万次。
  如果很少人下载应用程序，则其影响将是有限的。大约有10％的弗吉尼亚人使用Covidwise，这意味着他们碰到的人也有10％的机会也会使用它，因此理论上只有1％的近距离联系人被记录下来，从而限制了可以发送的暴露通知的潜在数量。
  “如果我要测试阳性，那我很高兴知道我有办法提醒人们我无法直接联系，”居住在弗吉尼亚州约克镇并使用Covidwise的凯利·德露西亚说。 “我认为，如果我们能够达到用户数量的临界水平，那将是抑制扩散的真正工具。”
  弗吉尼亚州州长拉尔夫·诺瑟姆（Ralph Northam）在新闻发布会上推广了该应用。当他9月下旬来到Covid-19时，他的三名工作人员收到了有关他们已被暴露的警报。他的新闻秘书艾琳娜·雅莫斯基（Alena Yarmosky）是其中三个人之一，这三人都曾与州长一起旅行，因此他们在收到智能手机警报之前就知道自己的暴露情况。
  雅莫斯基女士说，这是一次真实的测试，证明了该技术的有效性，并补充说，这可能已经使其他州长与不知道他的正面测试的州长发生了接触而引起其他人的注意。
其他弗吉尼亚人说，他们对该技术持怀疑态度。在阿灵顿在家工作的凯伦·麦克贝斯（Kellen MacBeth）说，他明白了为什么从事高风险工作的人可能想使用该应用程序，但他不知道为什么会需要它，因为他没有提早三个月离开家自从大流行以来，他的联系仅限于几个密友。他说，他试图避免下载应用程序来保护自己的隐私。
  弗吉尼亚卫生部可以查看Covidwise的下载频率，并且自8月下旬以来，它平均每天发送116条通知。但它不知道通知谁或他们住在哪里。两家公司表示，这些信息可以帮助公共卫生当局将资源引导到热点地区，但苹果和谷歌设计了该系统以使其模糊以保护隐私。
  弗吉尼亚州的两个Facebook本地团体提供了关于Covidwise的意见（就像面具一样）如何沿政治路线分裂的一瞥。其中一组针对主要支持乔·拜登的阿灵顿县居民，另一组针对汉诺威县蒙彼利埃的居民，后者更频繁地投票支持唐纳德·特朗普。阿灵顿县小组中有关Covidwise的帖子引起了很多积极的评价，即该应用程序可以帮助阻止该病毒的传播。蒙彼利埃（Montpelier）组中的类似帖子引起了更多负面评论，表明该应用将侵犯隐私。
  谷歌和苹果表示，这项技术可以保护人们的隐私。他们说，这些公司公开发布了源代码，以便可以验证索赔。
  一些弗吉尼亚人不使用Covidwise，因为他们不了解它。每周，数百名杂货店收银员，建筑工人和其他面临高风险的人出现在阿灵顿磨坊社区中心，以获取免费的Covid测试，那里没有任何推广该应用程序的迹象。最近一次拜访后离开中心的病人告诉《华尔街日报》记者，工作人员没有提及。
苹果和谷歌希望他们能够通过新版本解决贫乏的采用问题，该版本使各州避免开发自己的应用程序的时间和费用，并允许它们直接向智能手机用户发送推送警报以启用曝光通知。
  华盛顿特区于10月20日开始向居民和通勤者发出推送警报。到11月18日，约384,000个工作日人口的384,000人已在其智能手机上激活了这项技术。马里兰州和科罗拉多州也发出了推送警报，并且已经看到有近20％的人口在发布后的一个月内激活了该技术。
    如果链中的其他链接中断，则更广泛地采用曝光通知技术几乎没有用。瑞士政府Covid-19工作组的数字流行病学负责人MarcelSalathé说，瑞士在9月初看起来像是一个成功的故事，但那是在10月病例数跃升之前。 Salathé先生写了一篇论文，展示了SwissCovid应用程序的早期，有希望的数据。他说，测试可能需要几天的时间，有时甚至需要更长的时间才能获取代码，从而将通知延迟到无用的地步。
  Salathé先生说：“如果正确设置这些系统，将会有很大的不同。” “如果他们没有机会，那真是太可惜了。”
  您可以使用曝光通知应用程序吗？为什么或者为什么不？加入下面的对话。
note_en:
Covid-19 contact-tracing apps from  Apple    AAPL  -1.10%  and Google are coming to more states, along with evidence that they can help slow infections as long as enough people use them.
  Fourteen states and Washington, D.C., have recently adopted the “exposure notification” technology the companies built into their smartphone operating systems—Big Tech’s most significant contribution to the fight against Covid-19. California, and other states are piloting the technology and could release it soon. It lets people who have tested positive for Covid-19 alert others they have previously been in close contact with that they should take precautions or get a test.
  As  case counts explode and a nationwide  rollout of vaccines remains months away, epidemiologists say the technology can help slow the virus’s spread—if widely used and fast testing is broadly available. Yet many of the millions of Americans who have access so far are skeptical, suspicious or simply unaware of it.
  That has been the struggle in Virginia, which in early August became the first state to roll out an exposure notification app. Three months later, downloads of the app, known as Covidwise, amounted to roughly a 10th of Virginia’s population of about 8.5 million.
    Politicization of the virus has hampered efforts to increase the app’s adoption, said Jeff Stover, executive adviser to Virginia’s health commissioner who is in charge of Covidwise. “That doesn’t help convince Virginians to download and run an exposure notification app that is run by the government,” he said.
    Nearby Washington, D.C., offers some hope for public participation. A third of the district’s population, including commuters, had activated the smartphone function less than a month after it became available, thanks to a tweak from Apple and Google that allows public-health authorities to send push alerts that the technology is available. Colorado and Maryland also have seen fast uptake after sending push alerts.
  Mr. Stover said Virginia plans an update in a few weeks so it can send alerts and allow Covidwise to work with versions of the technology being run elsewhere, including nearby Washington, D.C., and Maryland.
  Many European countries rolled out apps based on the Apple-Google technology this summer. In Switzerland, 1,695 people called a special hotline after receiving a notification in the SwissCovid app that they had been exposed and 65 people who tested positive credited a notification from the app for encouraging them to get a test, according to  a paper published in September.
  Armin Betschart, an insurance executive who lives in a Zurich suburb, remembers a Monday afternoon in September when his SwissCovid app warned him of a possible exposure. The notification came a few hours before a family member called with news of a positive test, Mr. Betschart said.
  He tested negative but believes if he had been positive, and the exposure came from a stranger, the app would’ve been his only warning that he might be contagious before the onset of symptoms.
    Despite the promised benefits, few in the U.S. have downloaded the state-based apps or otherwise enabled the technology on their phones. About 800,000 people have downloaded Virginia’s app to date, Mr. Stover said. New York’s app has been downloaded roughly 700,000 times and Pennsylvania’s 500,000 times, according to data from research firm Sensor Tower.
  If few people download the apps, their impact will be limited. With about 10% of Virginians using Covidwise, that means there is a 10% chance the people they bump into are using it as well, so only 1% of close contacts are theoretically logged, limiting the potential number of exposure notifications that can be sent.
  “If I were to test positive, it would be good to know that I had a way to alert people that I have no way to contact directly,” said Kelly DeLucia, who lives in Yorktown, Va., and uses Covidwise. “I think that if we could reach critical mass with the number of users, it could be a real tool to help contain the spread.”
  Virginia’s governor, Ralph Northam, has promoted the app at news conferences. When he came down with Covid-19 in late September, three of his staff members received alerts that they had been exposed. All three had been traveling with the governor, so they were aware of their exposure before receiving the smartphone alert, according to his press secretary, Alena Yarmosky, who was one of the three.
  It was a real-world test that demonstrated the technology works, Ms. Yarmosky said, adding that it may have alerted others the governor came into contact with who didn’t know about his positive test.
    Other Virginians say they are skeptical of the technology. Kellen MacBeth, who works from home in Arlington, said he sees why people in high-risk jobs might want to use the app, but he doesn’t see why he would need it since he didn’t leave his house for three months early in the pandemic and has since limited his contacts to a few close friends. He tries to avoid downloading apps to protect his privacy, he said.
  The Virginia Department of Health can see how often Covidwise is downloaded, and it has sent 116 notifications a day, on average, since late August. But it doesn’t know who has been notified or where they live. That information could help public-health authorities direct resources to hot spots, but Apple and Google designed the system to obscure it to protect privacy, the companies have said.
  Two local Virginia Facebook groups offer a glimpse of how opinions about Covidwise, much like masks, are split along political lines.  One of the groups is for residents of Arlington County, which mostly supported Joe Biden, and  the other for residents of Montpelier in Hanover County, which voted more often for Donald Trump. A post about Covidwise in the Arlington County group elicited mostly positive comments that the app could help stop the virus’s spread. A similar post in the Montpelier group drew more negative comments, suggesting the app would invade privacy.
  Google and Apple say the technology protects people’s privacy. The companies posted the source code publicly so that claim can be verified, they say.
  Some Virginians don’t use Covidwise because they aren’t aware of it. Each week, hundreds of grocery store cashiers, construction workers and others facing high risk show up to get free Covid tests at the Arlington Mill Community Center, where there were no signs promoting the app. A patient exiting the center after a recent visit told a Wall Street Journal reporter that staff didn’t mention it.
      Apple and Google hope they can solve the problem of anemic adoption with a new release that lets states avoid the time and expense of developing their own apps and allows them to send push alerts directly to smartphone users to enable exposure notifications.
  Washington, D.C., began sending out push alerts to residents and commuters on Oct. 20. By Nov. 18, 384,000 people, roughly a third of its workday population, had activated the technology on their smartphones. Maryland and Colorado also sent push alerts and have seen almost 20% of their population activate the technology within a month of launch.
    Wider adoption of exposure notification technology is of little use if other links in the chain break. Switzerland looked like a success story in early September, but that was before case counts jumped in October, said Marcel Salathé, head of digital epidemiology for the Swiss government’s Covid-19 task force. Mr. Salathé wrote a paper showing early, promising data for the SwissCovid app. Testing can take days and getting the code sometimes even longer, delaying notifications to the point of uselessness, he said.
  “These systems can make quite a difference, if they are set up properly,” said Mr. Salathé. “It’s a shame when they aren’t given that chance.”
  Would you be comfortable using an exposure notification app? Why or why not? Join the conversation below.

note:
欺诈者在过去一周重定向了发往多个加密货币交易平台的电子邮件和网络流量。 KrebsOnSecurity了解到，针对GoDaddy（全球最大的域名注册商）的员工的欺诈活动助长了这些攻击。
  该事件是GoDaddy的最新一次入侵，它是依靠欺骗员工将所有权和/或对目标域的控制权转移给欺诈者。 3月份，针对GoDaddy支持员工的语音网络钓鱼诈骗使攻击者能够控制至少六个域名，包括交易经纪网站escrow.com。
 今年5月，GoDaddy透露，在2019年10月发生的安全事件（直到2020年4月才发现）之后，其28,000个客户的网络托管帐户遭到了入侵。
 这项最新的活动似乎已于11月13日或前后开始，攻击了加密货币交易平台liquid.com。
 Liquid首席执行官Kayamori在博客中说：“管理我们其中一个核心域名的域名托管服务提供商'GoDaddy'错误地将对帐户和域的控制权转移给了恶意参与者。 “这使演员可以更改DNS记录，进而控制许多内部电子邮件帐户。在适当的时候，恶意行为者能够部分破坏我们的基础架构，并获得对文档存储的访问权限。”
在11月18日中欧时间（CET）的清晨，cyptocurrency挖掘服务NiceHash发现，未经授权就更改了其在GoDaddy的域注册记录的某些设置，从而对该站点的电子邮件和网络流量进行了短暂重定向。 NiceHash冻结了大约24小时的所有客户资金，直到能够验证其域设置已更改回其原始设置。
 该公司在博客中写道：“目前看来，没有电子邮件，密码或任何个人数据被访问，但我们建议您重设密码并激活2FA安全性。”
 NiceHash的创始人Matjaz Skorjanc说，未经授权的更改是通过GoDaddy的Internet地址进行的，攻击者试图利用其对传入的NiceHash电子邮件的访问权对各种第三方服务（包括Slack和Github）执行密码重置。但是他说，GoDaddy当时无法实现，因为它正在经历广泛的系统故障，而电话和电子邮件系统无法响应。
 “我们几乎立即发现了这一点，并且开始减轻了这种攻击，” Skorjanc在给作者的电子邮件中说。 “幸运的是，我们与他们进行了良好的对抗，他们没有获得任何重要服务的机会。什么都没偷。”
 Skorjanc说，NiceHash的电子邮件服务已重定向到privateemail.com，这是另一个由大型域名注册商Namecheap Inc.经营的电子邮件平台。通过使用Farsight Security，该服务可将更改记录到一段时间内的域名记录中，KrebsOnSecurity指示该服务显示上周在GoDaddy注册的所有更改其电子邮件记录的域名，这些域名将其指向privateemail.com。然后根据Alexa.com将这些结果与前一百万个最受欢迎的网站进行了索引。
结果表明，同一组可能还针对了其他几种加密货币平台，包括Bibox.com，Celsius.network和Wirex.app。这些公司均未回应置评请求。
 在回答KrebsOnSecurity的问题时，GoDaddy承认在“数量有限”的GoDaddy员工因社交工程骗局而陷入困境之后，已经修改了“少数”客户域名。 GoDaddy表示，晚上7:00之间停运下午11:00 PST于11月17日与安全事件无关，但与计划中的网络维护过程中出现的技术问题有关。
 GoDaddy发言人Dan Race表示：“另外，与停机无关，对帐户活动的例行审核确定了对少数客户域和/或帐户信息的潜在未经授权的更改。” ”
 GoDaddy的声明继续说：“我们立即锁定了此事件所涉及的帐户，恢复了对帐户所做的所有更改，并协助受影响的客户重新获得对其帐户的访问权限。” “随着威胁行为者在攻击中变得越来越老练和更具攻击性，我们一直在向员工宣传可能用于对付他们的新策略，并采取新的安全措施来防止未来的攻击。”
 Race拒绝透露如何诱骗其员工进行未经授权的变更，并表示此事仍在调查中。但是，在今年早些时候影响escrow.com和其他GoDaddy客户域的攻击中，袭击者通过电话将员工作为目标，并能够读取GoDaddy员工留在客户帐户上的内部记录。
更重要的是，对escrow.com的攻击将网站重定向到了马来西亚的Internet地址，该地址托管的钓鱼域少于十二个，其中包括钓鱼网站servicenow-godaddy.com。这表明3月事件（甚至可能是最近一次事件）背后的攻击者通过呼叫GoDaddy员工并说服他们在欺诈性的GoDaddy登录页面上使用其员工凭据来成功。
 2020年8月，KrebsOnSecurity警告说，大型公司针对复杂的语音钓鱼或“钓鱼”欺诈的目标明显增加。专家说，由于正在进行的冠状病毒大流行，许多远程工作的员工极大地帮助了这些骗局的成功。
 典型的虚假骗局始于对目标组织远程工作的员工的一系列电话呼叫。网络钓鱼者通常会解释说，他们是从雇主的IT部门打电话来帮助解决公司电子邮件或虚拟专用网络（VPN）技术的问题。
 目的是说服目标对象通过电话泄露其凭据，或者在攻击者设置的模仿组织公司电子邮件或VPN门户的网站上手动输入凭据。
 7月15日，许多备受瞩目的Twitter帐户被用来发布一个比特币骗局，在几个小时内赚取了超过100,000美元。根据Twitter的说法，这种攻击之所以成功，是因为肇事者能够通过电话社交工程师数名Twitter员工，以放弃对内部Twitter工具的访问。
由联邦调查局和网络安全与基础设施安全局（CISA）联合发布的警报说，这些恶意攻击的肇事者使用社交媒体平台上的公共资料，招聘者和营销工具进行了大规模刮除，公开收集了目标公司员工的档案资料。后台检查服务和开源研究。
 FBI / CISA咨询包括公司可以实施的一些建议，以帮助减轻恶意攻击带来的威胁，其中包括：
 •使用硬件检查或已安装的证书之类的机制将VPN连接仅限制为受管设备，因此仅用户输入不足以访问公司VPN。
  •使用域监视来跟踪公司品牌域的创建或更改。
  •采用最小特权原则并实施软件限制策略或其他控制；监视授权用户的访问和使用情况。
•考虑对通过公用电话网络进行的员工之间的通信使用正式的身份验证过程，在讨论敏感信息之前，使用第二个因素对电话进行身份验证。
   •将正确的公司VPN URL标记为书签，并且不要仅凭入站电话访问其他URL。
 •怀疑来自声称来自合法组织的未知个人的不请自来的电话，访问或电子邮件。除非您确定某人有权使用该信息，否则请勿提供有关您的组织的个人信息或信息，包括其组织结构或网络。如果可能，请尝试直接与公司验证呼叫者的身份。
 •如果收到来电，请记录呼叫者的电话号码以及参与者试图将您发送给您的域，并将此信息转发给执法部门。
 •限制您在社交网站上发布的个人信息量。互联网是一种公共资源。仅发布任何人都能看到的信息。
•评估设置：站点可能会定期更改其选项，因此请定期检查您的安全性和隐私设置，以确保您的选择仍然合适。
note_en:
Fraudsters redirected email and web traffic destined for several cryptocurrency trading platforms over the past week. The attacks were facilitated by scams targeting employees at  GoDaddy, the world’s largest domain name registrar, KrebsOnSecurity has learned.
  The incident is the latest incursion at GoDaddy that relied on tricking employees into transferring ownership and/or control over targeted domains to fraudsters. In March, a voice phishing scam targeting GoDaddy support employees  allowed attackers to assume control over at least a half-dozen domain names, including transaction brokering site escrow.com.
 And in May of this year, GoDaddy  disclosed that 28,000 of its customers’ web hosting accounts were compromised following a security incident in Oct. 2019 that wasn’t discovered until April 2020.
 This latest campaign appears to have begun on or around Nov. 13, with an attack on cryptocurrency trading platform  liquid.com.
 “A domain hosting provider ‘GoDaddy’ that manages one of our core domain names incorrectly transferred control of the account and domain to a malicious actor,”  Liquid CEO Kayamori said in  a blog post. “This gave the actor the ability to change DNS records and in turn, take control of a number of internal email accounts. In due course, the malicious actor was able to partially compromise our infrastructure, and gain access to document storage.”
 In the early morning hours of Nov. 18 Central European Time (CET), cyptocurrency mining service  NiceHash disccovered that some of the settings for its domain registration records at GoDaddy were changed without authorization, briefly redirecting email and web traffic for the site. NiceHash froze all customer funds for roughly 24 hours until it was able to verify that its domain settings had been changed back to their original settings.
 “At this moment in time, it looks like no emails, passwords, or any personal data were accessed, but we do suggest resetting your password and activate 2FA security,” the company wrote  in a blog post.
 NiceHash founder  Matjaz Skorjanc said the unauthorized changes were made from an Internet address at GoDaddy, and that the attackers tried to use their access to its incoming NiceHash emails to perform password resets on various third-party services, including  Slack and  Github. But he said GoDaddy was impossible to reach at the time because  it was undergoing a widespread system outage in which phone and email systems were unresponsive.
 “We detected this almost immediately [and] started to mitigate [the] attack,” Skorjanc said in an email to this author. “Luckily, we fought them off well and they did not gain access to any important service. Nothing was stolen.”
 Skorjanc said NiceHash’s email service was redirected to  privateemail.com, an email platform run by  Namecheap Inc., another large domain name registrar. Using  Farsight Security, a service which maps changes to domain name records over time, KrebsOnSecurity instructed the service to show all domains registered at GoDaddy that had alterations to their email records in the past week which pointed them to privateemail.com. Those results were then indexed against the top one million most popular websites according to Alexa.com.
 The result shows that several other cryptocurrency platforms also may have been targeted by the same group, including  Bibox.com,  Celsius.network, and  Wirex.app. None of these companies responded to requests for comment.
 In response to questions from KrebsOnSecurity, GoDaddy acknowledged that “a small number” of customer domain names had been modified after a “limited” number of GoDaddy employees fell for a social engineering scam. GoDaddy said the outage between 7:00 p.m. and 11:00 p.m. PST on Nov. 17 was not related to a security incident, but rather a technical issue that materialized during planned network maintenance.
 “Separately, and unrelated to the outage, a routine audit of account activity identified potential unauthorized changes to a small number of customer domains and/or account information,” GoDaddy spokesperson  Dan Race said.  ”
 “We immediately locked down the accounts involved in this incident, reverted any changes that took place to accounts, and assisted affected customers with regaining access to their accounts,” GoDaddy’s statement continued. “As threat actors become increasingly sophisticated and aggressive in their attacks, we are constantly educating employees about new tactics that might be used against them and adopting new security measures to prevent future attacks.”
 Race declined to specify how its employees were tricked into making the unauthorized changes, saying the matter was still under investigation. But  in the attacks earlier this year that affected escrow.com and several other GoDaddy customer domains, the assailants targeted employees over the phone, and were able to read internal notes that GoDaddy employees had left on customer accounts.
 What’s more, the attack on escrow.com redirected the site to an Internet address in Malaysia that hosted fewer than a dozen other domains, including the phishing website  servicenow-godaddy.com. This suggests the attackers behind the March incident — and possibly this latest one — succeeded by calling GoDaddy employees and convincing them to use their employee credentials at a fraudulent GoDaddy login page.
 In August 2020, KrebsOnSecurity warned about a marked increase in large corporations  being targeted in sophisticated voice phishing or “vishing” scams. Experts say the success of these scams has been aided greatly by many employees working remotely thanks to the ongoing Coronavirus pandemic.
 A typical vishing scam begins with a series of phone calls to employees working remotely at a targeted organization. The phishers often will explain that they’re calling from the employer’s IT department to help troubleshoot issues with the company’s email or virtual private networking (VPN) technology.
 The goal is to convince the target either to divulge their credentials over the phone or to input them manually at a website set up by the attackers that mimics the organization’s corporate email or VPN portal.
 On July 15, a number of high-profile Twitter accounts were used  to tweet out a bitcoin scam that earned more than $100,000 in a few hours. According to Twitter, that attack succeeded because the perpetrators were able to social engineer several Twitter employees over the phone into giving away access to internal Twitter tools.
 An alert issued jointly by the  FBI and the  Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) says the perpetrators of these vishing attacks compile dossiers on employees at their targeted companies using mass scraping of public profiles on social media platforms, recruiter and marketing tools, publicly available background check services, and open-source research.
 The FBI/CISA advisory includes a number of suggestions that companies can implement to help mitigate the threat from vishing attacks, including:
 • Restrict VPN connections to managed devices only, using mechanisms like hardware checks or installed certificates, so user input alone is not enough to access the corporate VPN.
  • Employ domain monitoring to track the creation of, or changes to, corporate, brand-name domains.
  • Employ the principle of least privilege and implement software restriction policies or other controls; monitor authorized user accesses and usage.
 • Consider using a formalized authentication process for employee-to-employee communications made over the public telephone network where a second factor is used to authenticate the phone call before sensitive information can be discussed.
   • Bookmark the correct corporate VPN URL and do not visit alternative URLs on the sole basis of an inbound phone call.
 • Be suspicious of unsolicited phone calls, visits, or email messages from unknown individuals claiming to be from a legitimate organization. Do not provide personal information or information about your organization, including its structure or networks, unless you are certain of a person’s authority to have the information. If possible, try to verify the caller’s identity directly with the company.
 • If you receive a vishing call, document the phone number of the caller as well as the domain that the actor tried to send you to and relay this information to law enforcement.
 • Limit the amount of personal information you post on social networking sites. The internet is a public resource; only post information you are comfortable with anyone seeing.
 • Evaluate your settings: sites may change their options periodically, so review your security and privacy settings regularly to make sure that your choices are still appropriate.
title: 在黑客更改了许多加密交易平台的电子邮件和DNS记录之后，GoDaddy确认其一些员工因社交工程骗局而倒下
title_en: GoDaddy confirms some of its staff fell for a social engineering scam, after hackers changed the email and DNS records for a number of crypto trading platforms

note:
维多利亚州工党政府效仿了南澳大利亚自由党政府，并宣布从明年7月开始征收电动汽车道路税。
 维多利亚州财政部长蒂姆·帕拉斯（Tim Pallas）在周六下午宣布州政府2020/2021预算之前，宣布了对每公里征收的税收。维多利亚州和南澳大利亚州是世界上仅有的对电动汽车征税的辖区，而没有提供任何有意义的激励措施。
 维多利亚将对全电动汽车征收每公里2.5c的税，对插电式混合动力汽车征收每公里2美分的税。这相当于每年行驶20,000公里的电动汽车每年需要缴纳500美元的税，对于三菱欧蓝德这样的插件来说，则每年需要缴纳400美元的额外税。维多利亚预计每年筹集约3000万澳元。
 电动汽车行业内的一些人将此举视为各州的税收掠夺，希望在未来几年，如果以及何时电动汽车的普及成为联邦政府的主要组成部分，因为联邦燃料消费税的耗尽而夺取一些直接收入资源。市场。
 电动汽车理事会首席执行官Behyad Jafari将这一宣布描述为“可耻的”，并表示他曾多次试图与维多利亚州政府举行会议，但没有成功。
宣布的同一周，英国宣布将在2030年之前禁止新柴油和汽油车的销售，从而将其从2035年和2040年的发展向前推进，加利福尼亚紧随其后，欧盟可能将其拟议的禁令推迟到2025年，就像挪威一样。
 贾法里在推文中说：“我无法开始表达州政府的奇怪和令人失望。” “他们一直没有采取任何措施来激励电动汽车，但他们只花了几分钱就免除了电气化和脱碳方面的白旗。
 Jafari告诉The Driven：“（在澳大利亚，电动汽车的销售已经很困难。我们知道客户现在正在取消电动汽车的订单。”
   “地球上没有其他国家认为对电动汽车征收特别的新税是明智的。继续燃烧会污染空气的外国石油是没有道理的。我们为什么要征收不必要的新税，以减缓向更清洁，更健康的空气的过渡？ “
 电动汽车行业认为，随着车队向电动汽车过渡，道路使用者收费是不可避免的，但他认为应该公平，公平地实施收费。它指出，汽油消费税进入了一般收入，而不是道路资金，而且由于来自消费税，印花税和豪华车税的增加，电动汽车的所有者要比汽油的汽车所有者支付更多的税款。
“这项新税收是建立在神话之上的。燃油消费税收入不会被隔离在公路上，从长远来看将会减少。但是随着我们不再使用汽油和柴油，与空气污染和与气候变化相关的其他成本相关的疾病也将减少。”贾法里说。
 电动汽车税最热烈的支持者是澳大利亚基础设施合作组织，该组织在周六的一份声明中庆祝了“百万富翁税”。
 IPA首席执行官阿德里安·德怀尔（Adrian Dwyer）说：“现在，马自达或起亚的家庭正在为使用道路付费，而电动特斯拉，保时捷或捷豹的百万富翁可以免费乘车，这是不公平的。”道路使用者充电器。
 “电动汽车……不要悬浮，它们应该像其他所有人一样付费使用道路。”
 帕拉斯回应了这些评论，在周六告诉记者，“驾驶Hilux ute的传统轿车”支付燃油消费税是不公平的，而驾驶特斯拉的人则避免使用燃油消费税，因为他们不使用汽油。
IPA的声明在许多方面都具有误导性。它忽略了许多以不到20,000美元的价格购买二手Nissan Leafs并且肯定不是百万富翁的人。它忽略了混合型雷克萨斯汽车中的百万富翁，而这种汽车很少以燃油消费的方式支付费用。
 人们认为，IPA是代表大型道路建设者和收费公路运营商（例如Transurban）的“智囊团”，在游说州政府推动电动车道路税方面发挥了作用。
 人们认为新南威尔士州也将在其预算中引入电动汽车税，但它大吃一惊，财政部长唐·佩罗特（Dom Perrottet）只是在谈论在未来12个月内阁提出一项提案。对南澳大利亚州对其电动汽车税采取的强烈反应，以及在遭到州工党，绿党和独立党反对后，其在州议会中的失败，可能使它感到恐惧。
 贾法里（Jafari）说，过去12个月，维多利亚州财政部多次要求他与财务主任会面，告诉他电动汽车不是优先事项，他说，征税的宣布表明人们对电动汽车的作用缺乏了解。发挥减排作用。
 贾法里说：“各州目前还没有达到减少交通运输部门碳排放量的轨道，交通运输部门是第二大排放源。” “他们不会实现净零排放，而今天他们已经明确了这一点。”
如南澳大利亚那样，每公里道路使用费为美分，这意味着维多利亚州政府将绕开联邦燃油税。但是，在澳大利亚的道路上只有大约20,000辆电动汽车，而在维多利亚州，电动汽车的份额可能只有四分之一，因此选择的范围将很小。
 贾法里说：“从长远来看，他们认为这是摆脱公路消费税的一种方式，但是对于州减少碳排放和增加电动汽车使用的能力，这没有任何考虑。”
 电动汽车理事会已发布报告，重点介绍了电动汽车可为经济和社会做出的财务，环境和健康益处，各州也进行了自己的研究，并得出了相同的结果。
 联邦电动汽车的前景更加令人困惑。尽管预计联邦电动汽车战略将于2020年中发布，但在2019年发布电动汽车参议院调查的结果后18个月，随着2020年结束，这一迹象仍然没有迹象表明。
 贾法里说，有消息称现在可能推迟到2021年初，但与州零排放路线图一样，它可能只会包括充电基础设施投资。
贾法里（Jafari）说，如果有动力的话，消费者最终将选择购买电动汽车，例如在ACT中刚刚引入了免费注册和零利率贷款以鼓励购买。
 联邦和州政府的贾法里说：“他们拒绝对话。” “客户不会四处思考基础设施（那是他们看不到的地方）–钱才是钱包里重要的钱。”
note_en:
The Victorian Labor government has followed the example of the  South Australia Liberal government and announced the imposition of an electric vehicle road tax from July next year.
 The tax – to be imposed on a cents per kilometre basis, was annouced by Victoria treasurer Tim Pallas on Saturday afternoon, ahead of the state’s 2020/2021 budget on Tuesday. Victoria and South Australia are the only jurisdictions in the world to impose such a tax on EVs, without providing any meaningul incentive.
 Victoria will impose the tax of 2.5c a kilometre for full electric vehicles, and 2 cents a kilometre for plug in hybrid vehicles. That translates to an annual tax of $500 for an electric car that travels 20,000kms a year, or an extra $400 a year tax for a plug in like the Mitsubishi Outlander. Victoria expects to raise around $30 million a year.
 The move has been seen by some within the EV industry as a tax grab by the states, hoping to seize some direct revenue resources as the federal fuel excise tax depletes in future years if, and when, the uptake of EVs becomes a major part of the market.
 Behyad Jafari, CEO of the Electric Vehicle Council, described the announcement as “shameful” and said he had repeatedly tried to obtain meetings with the Victoria government without success.
 The announcements come in the same week that the  UK announced it would ban the sale of new diesel and petrol cars by 2030, bringing the move forward from 2035 and 2040, with  California following suit and the  EU possibly bringing forward its proposed ban to 2025, as Norway is doing.
 “I can’t begin to express how strange and disappointing this is from state governments,” Jafari said in a Tweet. “Having made no move to incentivise electric vehicles, they have waived the white flag on electrification and decarbonisation for a few measly bucks.
 “(Selling EVs” is already a hard sell in Australia,” Jafari told The Driven. “We know customers are now cancelling orders on electric vehicles.”
   “No other nation on Earth has thought it sensible to apply a special new tax to electric vehicles. It makes no sense to keep burning foreign oil that clogs our air with pollution. Why would we slow the transition to cleaner and healthier air by imposing a new and unnecessary tax? “
 The EV industry accepts that a road user charge is inevitable as the car fleets transition to electric, but argue it should be introduced fairly and evenly. It points out the petrol excise goes into general revenue rather than road funding, and EV owners pay more tax than petrol car owners due to higher taxes from GST, stamp duty, and luxury car tax.
 “This new tax is built on a myth. Fuel excise income is not quarantined for roads and will drop in the long run. But as we shift away from petrol and diesel, diseases linked to air pollution and other costs associated with climate change will also decrease,” Jafari said.
 The most enthusiastic proponent of an EV tax has been the Infrastructure Partnerships Australia, which celebrated the “tax on millionaire” car drivers in a statement on Saturday.
 “It is not fair right now that a family in a Mazda or a Kia is paying to use the roads while a millionaire in an elecgtric Tesla, Porsche or Jaguar gets a free ride,” said IPA CEO Adrian Dwyer, who wrote a thesis on road user chargers.
 “Electric vehicles … don’t levitate, and they should be paying to use the roads like everyone else.”
 Pallas echoed those comments, telling reporters on Saturday that it was not fair that “a tradie driving a Hilux ute” paid fuel excise, while those driving a Tesla avoid it because they do not use petrol.
 The statement from the IPA is misleading on many fronts. It ignores the many people who have bought second hand Nissan Leafs for less than $20,000 and who are certainly not millionaires. And it ignores the millionaires in hybrid Lexus cars and the like that pay little in the way of fuel excise.
 It is believed that the IPA, a “think tank” which represents big road builders and toll road operator such as Transurban, has been instrumental in lobbying the state government to push for the EV road tax.
 It was thought that NSW was also going to introduce an EV tax in its budget, but it baulked and Treasurer Dom Perrottet only talked of taking a proposal to Cabinet in the next 12 months. It may have been spooked by the furious response to the South Australia move to its EV tax, and  its likely defeat in state parliament after it was opposed by state Labor, the Greens and independents.
 Jafari says that he was told multiple times in the past 12 months by the Victorian treasury when seeking a meeting with the treasurer that electric vehicles were not a priority, and says the announcement of a tax shows a lack of understanding about the role electric vehicles can play in reducing emissions.
 “States are already not on track to meet carbon emissions reduction in the transport sector, which is the second biggest producer of emissions,” says Jafari. “They’re not going to achieve net zero emissions, and they’ve made that clear today.”
  By introducing a cents per kilometre road usage fee, as in South Australia, would mean the Victorian government would bypass the federal fuel excise. But with only 20,000 or so electric vehicles on Australian roads, and perhaps a quarter of these in Victoria, the pickings will be slim.
 “They think in the long term it’s a way to take road excise away from federal government, but there is no regard for what this will do for states’ ability to reduce carbon emissions and increase EV uptake,” says Jafari.
 The Electric Vehicle Council has issued reports that  highlight the financial, environmental and health benefits that electric vehicles can contribute to the economy and society, and states have done their own research with the same results.
 The outlook for electric vehicles at a federal level is even more perplexing. While a federal EV strategy was expected to be released in mid-2020, 18 months after the findings of an EV senate enquiry were released in 2019, there is still no sign of it as 2020 reaches an end.
 Word is that may now be put off until early 2021, but as with state zero emissions roadmaps it will likely only include charging infrastructure investments, says Jafari.
 Jafari says consumers will ultimately make choices to buy electric vehicles if there are incentives to do so – such as  in the ACT which has just introduced free registration and a zero interest loan to encourage uptake.
 “They’re turning down the dialogue,” says Jafari of the federal and state governments. “Customers don’t walk around thinking about infrastructure [that is somewhere they cannot see] – it’s the money that’s what is in their wallets that matters.”
title: “可耻的”：维多利亚跟随南澳大利亚并征收电动汽车道路税
title_en: “Shameful”: Victoria follows South Australia and imposes electric car road tax

note:
1621年3月22日，一个美国原住民代表团走过了现在新英格兰南部，与一群接管了最近荒芜的印度定居点的外国人会面。党的领袖是一个不安的胜利者：Wampanoag联邦的政党Sachem（政治军事领导人），一个由几十个村庄组成的松散联盟，控制着马萨诸塞州的东南大部分地区。萨摩塞特（Samoset），北方同盟军的萨赫姆；马萨苏特（Massasoit）勉强带来的口译员Tisquantum是一位不信任的俘虏。
    马萨索特（Massasoit）是位机敏的政客，但他所面临的困境将考验马基雅维利（Machiavelli）。大约五年前，他的大多数臣民都遭受了可怕的灾难。整个村庄都被人口减少了。 Massasoit可以竭尽全力将他的人民的残余遗留在一起。除了他的问题外，这场灾难还没有触及Wampanoag的长期敌人，即西方的Narragansett联盟。马萨索特（Massasoit）担心，很快他们会利用Wampanoag的弱点来压倒他们。他唯一能看到的解决方案就是充满自己的危险，因为它牵扯到外国人，也就是跨海人民。
  欧洲人访问新英格兰至少一个世纪。苍白的外国人比土著人矮一些，衣着奇特，经常脏得难以忍受，苍白苍苍的外国人有着奇特的蓝眼睛，那双明亮的动物般的头发从脸庞中冒出来。他们脾气暴躁，脾气暴躁，容易受到鞭打的折磨，而且常常令人惊讶地无法胜任印第安人看来像基本任务的事情。但是他们也制造了有用和美观的商品，例如铜水壶，闪闪发光的彩色玻璃，钢刀和斧头，这与新英格兰地区的其他商品不同。而且，他们会用这些有价值的物品换成印第安人用作毯子的廉价皮草。
  随着时间的流逝，Wampanoag和新英格兰沿海地区的其他土著社会一样，已经学会了如何管理欧洲的存在。他们鼓励货物交换，但只允许游客短暂停留在岸上，进行精心控制的游览。那些坚决反对的人被强力提醒了印度好客的时间有限。同时，Wampanoag抵挡了印第安人的内战，阻止他们直接与外国人交易。通过这种方式，海岸线团体将自己置于经典的中间商的位置，监督欧洲获得印度产品的渠道和印度获得欧洲产品的渠道。现在，为了扭转长期政策，马萨苏特决定允许新移民无限期逗留，前提是他们与瓦潘诺格正式联手反对纳拉甘西特。
  口译员Tisquantum一年半前在马萨索特的家中出现。他说一口流利的英语，因为他在英国生活了几年。但马萨索特（Massasoit）担心，在危机中，Tisquantum可能会与外国人并驾齐驱。萨莫塞特（Sameset）的第三名成员-出现在几周前，他从缅因州的家中乘坐一艘英国船驶向海岸。因为萨莫塞特语也说一点英语，所以马萨索特首先派他而不是Tisquantum与外国人见面。
1621年3月17日，萨莫塞特无人陪伴，手无寸铁，走进了英国人居住的粗鲁小屋中。殖民者看到一个健壮，挺拔的人只穿缠腰布。他的直发被剃掉了，但从后肩流下来。令他们惊讶的是，这个几乎赤裸的男人用残破但可以理解的英语向他们打招呼。第二天早上他带着几件礼物离开了，第二天回来，他带着五个“高大的男人”（用殖民者爱德华·温斯洛的话说）回来，脸上有三英寸的黑色条纹。双方进行了不确定性的交谈，彼此检查了几个小时。
  现在，在22日，在马萨苏特（Massasoit）和其他印度公司不可见的情况下，萨莫塞特（Samoset）和提斯昆腾（Tisquantum）走进了外国人摇摇欲坠的基地。他们与殖民者交谈了大约一个小时。然后，马萨索特（Massasoit）和印度党其他成员突然出现在附近一条小溪的山顶上，在一条小溪的两岸。惊慌失措的是，欧洲人撤退到溪流另一侧的小山上，在那里他们将几门大炮安置在半成品的寨子后面。僵局随之而来。
  最终，温斯洛表现出决定性，后来导致他被选为殖民地州长。他穿着全套防弹服，背着剑，在溪流中涉水并自a为人质。 Massasoit的兄弟接管了Winslow，然后Massasoit自己越过水面，随后是Tisquantum和Massasoit的20个人，全部没有武装。殖民者把萨赫姆带到一栋未完成的房子里，给了他一些靠垫。双方分享了一些外国人自制的月光，并定下来聊天，Tisquantum翻译。
  马萨索特（Massasoit）穿着和他的同事们一样的鹿皮披肩和绑腿，并且像他们一样，用驱虫剂的油和带红色的紫色染料遮住了脸。在他的脖子上挂着一袋烟，一把长刀和一串厚实的珍贵的白色贝壳珠，叫做wampum。从外观上看，温斯洛后来写道：“他是一个非常精力充沛的人，在他最好的岁月中，身体强健，面容沉重，无言以对。”上个冬天几乎没有幸存下来的欧洲人的状况要差得多。现在，原始殖民地的一半位于地下，上面绘有死者头部的木制标记；大多数幸存者营养不良。 Wampanoag与英国殖民者之间的会晤标志着美国历史上的关键时刻。 “一个友好的印度人”
    外国人把他们的殖民地称为普利茅斯。他们自己是著名的朝圣者。随着学童的学习，朝圣者在那次会议上获得了Tisquantum（通常称为Squanto）的服务。在1970年代，当我上高中时，一个受欢迎的历史著作是《美国：人民与价值观》。殖民色彩丰富的插图之中，是Tisquantum角色的简洁解释：
一个友好的印第安人斯宽托（Squanto）帮助了殖民者。他向他们展示了如何种植玉米以及如何在荒野的边缘生活。一名士兵，迈尔斯·斯坦迪什上尉，教给朝圣者如何防御不友善的印第安人。
  我的老师解释说，玉米是朝圣者所不熟悉的，而Squanto展示了正确的种植方式-将种子粘在一小堆泥土中，并伴以豆类和南瓜，然后将它们缠绕在高大的茎上。他告诉朝圣者，将鱼埋在玉米种子旁边，使土壤肥沃。我的老师说，按照这个建议，殖民者种植了如此多的玉米，使玉米成为了第一个感恩节的核心。我们以生硬的方式记笔记。
  就美国而言，它的人民和价值观并没有错。但是它给人的印象是完全误导的。
  Tsquantum对殖民地的生存至关重要。在关键的会议之后，他移居普利茅斯，并度过了余生。在那段时间里，他确实教过朝圣者农业方法，尽管一些考古学家认为Tisquantum从欧洲农民那里汲取了鱼肥的想法，自那以后就一直使用该技术中世纪。但是美国：它的人民和价值观永远无法解释为什么他如此热情地帮助入侵他的祖国的人们。在篇幅有限的书中，跳过这种复杂性是可以理解的。然而，缺乏关注是更大的考虑失败的征兆，甚至没有考虑到印度人可能有动机。
  与普利茅斯谈判达成的马萨苏伊特同盟也是如此。从印度的角度来看，他为什么这样做？从短期Wampanoag的角度来看，该联盟是成功的，因为它帮助推迟了Narragansett。但是从新英格兰整个印度社会的角度来看，这是一场灾难，因为它确保了普利茅斯殖民地的生存，而普利茅斯殖民地率领了英国移民到新英格兰的大潮。所有这些不仅在我的高中教科书中没有，而且在它们所依据的学术著作中都没有。
这种疏忽可以追溯到朝圣者自己，他们将朝圣者归因于缺乏对上帝旨意的有效本地抵抗。殖民者丹尼尔·古金（Daniel Gookin）写道：“神圣的天意”赞成“英国人安静而和平的解决办法”。后来的作家倾向于将欧洲的成功归因于欧洲的技术。历史学家说，在只有一方拥有步枪和大炮的比赛中，另一方的动机是无关紧要的。到19世纪末，在美国崛起的传奇中，东北印第安人被认为是迅速褪色的背景细节，“最终沦为失败者的边缘人”，威廉和玛丽干脆地接受了我的采访。越南战争时期对朝圣者的谴责是帝国主义或种族主义，只是以一种新形式复制了这一错误。不管原因是朝圣者上帝，朝圣者的枪支还是朝圣者的贪婪，都是当地人的损失。按照这种观点，印第安人不可能停止殖民，他们几乎没有尝试过。
  但是，从1970年代开始，历史学家对这种观点不满意。史密斯学院的历史学家尼尔·索尔兹伯里（Neal Salisbury）告诉我：“印度人被视为琐事，毫无效果。” “但是这种假设-整个大陆都是蛋糕-根本没有道理。”索尔兹伯里（Salisbury）和其他研究人员试图通过殖民记录来窥视印度人的生活。他们的工作使人们对当土著人和新移民之间以相对平等的方式面对面时的互动产生了强烈的疑问。
  索尔兹伯里说：“回顾历史记录，很明显印度人正试图控制自己的命运。” “他们常常成功了”-就像所有人一样，他们都知道后果不是他们所期望的。
    Tisquantum不是他出生时的名字。在东北那部分地区，后顾之忧指的是愤怒，尤其是manitou的愤怒，manitou是全世界印第安人宗教信仰中心的，令人震惊的精神力量。当Tisquantum走近朝圣者并通过那个神语识别自己时，好像他伸出了手，说，你好，我是上帝的愤怒。
  Tisquantum也不认为自己是“印度人”，就像今天同一地区的居民自称为“西半球人”一样。正如Tisquantum的后来历史所表明的那样，他首先将自己视为Patuxet的公民，Pattexet是构成Wampanoag联邦的现在马萨诸塞州东部和罗德岛的十几个海岸线定居点之一。反过来，Wampanoag是与Nauset结盟的一部分，Nauset由鳕鱼角和马萨诸塞州的30个小组组成，马萨诸塞州有几十个村庄聚集在马萨诸塞湾附近。所有这些人都说了马萨诸塞州（Algonquian）语言家族的成员的变体，该家族当时是北美东部最大的。在马萨诸塞州，新英格兰海岸的名称是黎明之地，太阳升起的地方。黎明国的居民是第一光的人民。
一万年前，当中美洲和秘鲁的印第安人发明农业并合并成村庄时，新英格兰几乎没有人居住，这是一个极好的理由，直到最近，新英格兰才被一英里厚的冰原所覆盖。随着床单的退缩，人们缓慢地进入，尽管该地区长期处于寒冷和令人不快的状态，尤其是沿海岸线。由于不断上升的海平面不断淹没海岸，因此湿软的科德角直到大约公元前1000年才完全锁定其当代形态。到那个时候，黎明国已经发展成为更具吸引力的东西：湿润的枫树林的生态疯狂棉被，潮间带壳的贝类布满潮汐河口，高地树林密布，蔓越莓和兰花的苔藓沼泽，沙洲和海滩的复杂咆哮以及火w的架子用生态历史学家威廉·克农（William Cronon）的话说，“松树”是“即使在几英里的罗盘内也能提供巨大的多样性”。
  到公元第一个千年结束时，农业迅速传播，该地区正成为各式各样的社区，每个社区都有其偏爱的地形，生存方式和文化风格。散布在寒冷的高地的许多湖泊，池塘和沼泽上的是小型且流动的猎人和采集者。他们中的大多数人最近或即将采用农业，但种植的农作物仍是次要食物，是对土地野生产品的补充。相比之下，新英格兰的主要河流山谷拥有庞大的永久性村庄，许多村庄都坐落在郊区的小村庄和狩猎营地中。由于玉米，豆类和南瓜遍布广阔的土地，每个住所都围绕着这些定居点，这些定居点沿着康涅狄格州，查尔斯和其他河流山谷蔓延了数英里，一个城镇与另一个城镇碰撞。沿着Tisquantum和Massasoit居住的海岸，村庄往往更小，更宽松，尽管永久性也不少。
  与高地猎人不同，在河流和海岸线上的印第安人并未在陆地上漫游。大多数海岸线家庭将向内陆步行15分钟，以避免直接暴露于冬季风暴和潮汐中。每个村庄都有自己独特的耕作和觅食混合方式-一个毗邻牡蛎床的地方可能纯粹是为了种玉米而种植玉米，而仅几英里远的一个村庄可能几乎完全依靠其收成为生，每年秋天都充满了地下储藏坑。威廉和玛丽学院的人类学家凯瑟琳·布拉格登（Kathleen J. Bragdon）写道，每个社区都在不断地“像水银一样在其边界内以一种流动的方式加入和分裂”。她说，这样的定居点“在考古或人类学文献中没有名字”。
    在Wampanoag联盟中，这些流银社区之一是Patuxet，Tisquantum出生于16世纪末。 Patuxet藏在科德角（Cape Cod）湾的广阔地段，坐在一个小港口上方的低矮建筑上，被沙洲竖起了锯齿，而且很浅，孩子们可以从海滩上数百码处走入水中，直到到达头顶。在西部，玉米丘以平行的行列穿过沙丘。在田野之外，距海一英里或更远的地方，长满了一片橡树，栗子和山胡桃树的森林，开阔又像公园，灌木丛被专家每年一次的燃烧所压倒。正如一位英国游客所说的那样，“令人愉悦的空气和前景”，Patuxet“一年中每天都有很多鱼和禽类。”大量产卵的大西洋鲑鱼，短鼻st，条纹鲈鱼和美洲sha鱼充满了港口。但是最重​​要的鱼类收获是在春季末期，那​​时像鲱鱼一样的刺夫蜂拥而至，穿过村庄的快速而浅浅的溪流成群。
  Tisquantum的童年Wetu（家）是由拱形的杆子绑在一起组成的，拱形的圆顶在冬天被紧密编织的草皮覆盖，在夏天被薄板栗树皮覆盖。大火不断在中央燃烧，烟雾从屋顶的一个小孔冒出来。殖民者威廉·伍德叹了口气，韦图的多层垫子困住了空气的绝热层，“比英国人的房子还要温暖”。它也比典型的英式蜡笔涂抹屋泄漏少。伍德并未掩饰他对印度垫子的方式的钦佩之情“尽管雨水既猛烈又漫长，但拒绝任何雨滴进入。”
房屋的边缘是低矮的床，有时足够宽，整个家庭都可以躺在这里。它们通常是从地面抬起约一英尺的平台式，并堆满了垫子和毛皮。年轻的Tisquantum在火光下睡觉时，会盯着from子上悬挂的麻袋和树皮箱的阴影。声音会在黑暗中ski绕：一个人唱摇篮曲，然后另一个人唱，直到每个人都睡着了。早晨，当他醒来时，大的蛋形锅玉米和土豆泥将着火，用肉，蔬菜或干鱼煮，以慢炖晚餐。在外面，他会听到巨大的研钵和杵的轰鸣声，在那儿妇女将玉米干碾碎成nokake，一种面粉状的粉末，“是如此甜美，富牙利，而且丰盛，”殖民者古金惊叹道，“印度人将在这里旅行很多天。除了这顿饭。”根据一项现代的重建，当时的曙光饮食平均每天约2500卡热量，高于饥荒的欧洲。
  朝圣者作家普遍报道说，Wampanoag的家庭亲密而充满爱心，有人认为这比英国的家庭更为亲密。那时的欧洲人倾向于认为儿童从7岁左右就从婴儿期发展到成年，因此常常将他们送出去工作。相比之下，印度父母将青春期之前的岁月视为一个好玩的发展时期，他们一直将后代留在身边直到结婚。像Tisquantum这样的男孩在乡下探索，在海港南端的池塘里游泳，并用一个小皮球踢足球。在夏季和秋季，他们在田间小屋里露营，除草玉米并赶走鸟类。射箭术始于2岁。到了青春期，男孩们将进行射击和躲避箭的游戏。
  黎明国教育的主要目标是塑造个性。人们期望男人和女人勇敢，强壮，诚实和无怨无悔。闲聊和八卦被皱眉了。伍德报道说：“说话少而又恰如其分的人是他们唯一爱的人，”印度男孩成年后，他们独自在森林里度过了整个冬天，只装备了弓，斧头和小刀。伍德补充说，这些方法行之有效。 “殴打他们，鞭打他们，捏他们，猛击他们，如果[印第安人]下定决心不退缩，他们就不会。”
  根据史密斯学院的索尔兹伯里（Salisbury）的说法，Tisquantum的养生方法可能比他的朋友们更为严格，因为看来他被选为普尼人，是萨赫姆的顾问-保镖。为了掌握忽略疼痛的技巧，准入者必须接受光秃秃地穿梭于荆棘丛中的经历。他们经常禁食以学习自律。在树林里度过冬天之后，pniese候选人再次接受了另一项测试：饮用苦龙胆汁直到呕吐，然后一遍又一遍地重复这一过程。
  Patuxet和它的邻近定居点一样，由萨赫姆人统治，他们执行法律，谈判条约，控制外国联系，进贡，宣布战争，为寡妇和孤儿提供食物并分配耕地。 Patuxet Sachem忠于西南Wampanoag村的伟大sachem，并通过他效忠了科德角Nauset联盟和波士顿附近的Massachusett联盟的sachem。同时，Wampanoag是西部的Narragansett和Pequots以及北部的Abenaki的竞争对手和敌人。
16世纪的新英格兰居住着100,000或更多的土著人，这个数字正在缓慢增加。他们中的大多数人生活在海岸线社区，那里越来越多的人开始将农业从选择权变为必需品。这些更大的定居点需要更集中的管理；尽管不稀缺，但需要管理好土地和产卵小溪等自然资源。结果，群体之间的界限变得更加正式。萨凯姆斯拥有更多的力量和更多的防御能力，因此更加相互对抗。政治紧张局势一直持续。沿海和里
